narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 36, and your decile rankings from early and later years in school—both at the third decile, which are below the typical fifth or sixth decile for most applicants. It also noted your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, the model estimated a 61.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking, as being in the lower third of your class signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance through law school. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which, while solid at 36, fell slightly short of the average 36.7 and pulled somewhat in the direction of passing but not enough to offset other concerns. Your first-year decile ranking, also in the lower third compared to the average around the sixth, further reinforced the risk by highlighting early academic hurdles. In contrast to typical students who often rank higher in these deciles and edge out on standardized tests, your profile clustered more with those who struggled on the bar. This combination made the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than average. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the typical 36.7, strengthening your grades to climb into a higher school decile, or gaining targeted experience like internships that demonstrate legal aptitude. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted probability favorably. You're clearly motivated—many applicants in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on these levers. Keep pushing; law school doors can reopen with refined preparation.
